author: @Savagedownsouth
text:
I don't think it would be useful for much, but that looks like a very easy system to balance. In fact, it looks very similar to the vee-bearing balancing systems used in motor shops and factories a long time ago. They challenge with those was always to get the bearings as frictionless as possible, which i think this design neatly solves!

If you want to do do a simple balancing, just spin the shaft a few times, mark the topside with a sharpie when it stops spinning, and the bottom side is the heavy one. Add or subtract weight to even it out. You could probably spin the shaft much faster and steadier just from that.

Better forms of balancing require a "floating" carriage, that the unbalanced shaft can shake when it spins. The shake of the shaft is generally measured by mechanical sensors or indicators in contact with the carriage, which is a problem here, because the shaft doesn't contact the carriage. The shaft doesn't need to contact a floating carriage though, because the shaft itself floats! First time I've seen that, in all my years of balancing mechanical parts. 

The shaft could be balanced at higher speeds by marking both ends of the shaft with different colored paints, evenly spaced around the circumference, spinning the shaft faster and faster until the whole thing starts to wobble, then hitting the whole thing with an adjustable strobe light. Adjust the strobe until the shaft looks like a still image, and you'll see one of the colors sticks out further from centerline than the others. That's the new heavy spot. Balance with weights, or a drill, or even dabs of hot glue, and then spin the fan faster until it wobbles again. Repeat.

Realistically, there's an upper limit to how balanced something can be. At certain speeds the shaft and fan will be affected by turbulence, the flex of the propeller blades, and weird resonances. In systems with fixed bearings, sometimes those speeds are surpassable, and only cause a slight hum or vibration while getting up to speed. In this system, the shaft and fan will probably fly across the room!

I bet you can get it screaming fast before that happens though.

Thanks for sharing! I used to watch your channel in college, when i was getting into electroplating. Now I'm a heavy machinist that repairs worn out bearing journals on large motors and dynamos.
